coredata.dc:title: Nuclear-targeted of TAT peptide-conjugated carbon dots for both one-and two-photon fluorescence imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yukun
Li, Xintong
Cong, Shuang
Zhao, Haidong
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with both one- and two-photon fluorescence have attracted much attention in the field of bioimaging. In this study, we reported a strategy for the design of peptide-conjugated CDs for cellular nuclear-targeted imaging. The CDs were prepared from amino acid and formic acid via an one-step hydrothermal synthesis method, and then modified with trans-activator of transcription (TAT) peptide for both one- and two- photon nuclear-targeted fluorescence imaging. The CDs derived from tryptophan (Trp) and formic acid showed the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 58.4% with an average size about 1.7 nm in diameter. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the Trp/formic acid CDs were 360 and 442 nm, respectively. The Trp/formic acid CDs were successfully conjugated with TAT peptide through the reaction between the amino group of the peptide and carboxylic group of the CDs. Upon grafting with TAT peptide, the resulted TAT-Trp/formic acid CDs could be used as nuclear-targeted fluorescent probes for both one- and two-photon live cell fluorescence imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
TAT peptide
Two-photon
Nuclear-targeted imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence stability of CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31872915 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-photon fluorescence spectra of Trp/formic acid CDs (a), Trp/formaldehyde CDs (c) and Trp/acetic acid CDs (e). Two-photon fluorescence spectra of Trp/formic acid CDs (b), Trp/formaldehyde CDs (d) and Trp/acetic acid CDs (f).

(a) XPS spectra for Trp/formic acid CDs, inset shows the high resolution C1s peak. (b) Fluorescence decay curve of Trp/formic acid CDs using a 376 nm laser as excitation source. (c) pH effect on fluorescence intensity of Trp/formic acid CDs (λex = 360, λem = 442 nm). (d) Photostability of Trp/formic acid CDs using fluorescein of CDs as a control.
TEM images of Trp/formic acid CDs (a) and TAT-CDs (c). Particle size distribution histograms of the Trp/formic acid CDs (b) TAT-CDs (d).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of TAT-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Trp/formic acid CDs and TAT-CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of TAT-CDs (1), TAT peptides (2) and Trp/formic acid CDs (3).

One-photon cell imaging of mouse melanoma B16-F10 cancer cells under (a) bright field, excitation of (b) 405 nm, (c) 488 nm, (d) 543 nm and (e) merger image of (b), (c) and (d) with Trp/formic acid-CDs. One-photon cell imaging of mouse melanoma B16-F10 cancer cells under (f) bright field, excitation of (g) 405 nm, (h) 488 nm, (i) 543 nm and (j) merger of (g), (h) and (i) with TAT-CDs. One-photon cell imaging of mouse melanoma B16-F10 cancer cells under (k) bright field, excitation of (l) 405 nm, (m) 488 nm, (n) 543 nm and (o) merger of (l), (m) and (n) stained with PI. Scale bar = 20 μm.

One-photon cell imaging of B16-F10 cancer cells with TAT-CDs under 405 nm excitation for (a) 0 h, (b) 2 h, (c) 4 h, (d) 8 h, (e) 24 h, 488 nm excitation for (f) 0 h, (g) 2 h, (h) 4 h, (i) 8 h, (j) 24 h with TAT-CDs, and 543 nm excitation for (k) 0 h, (l) 2 h, (m) 4 h, (n) 8 h, (o) 24 h with PI. Overlay images of 405 nm, 488 nm and 543 nm for (p) 0 h, (q) 2 h, (r) 4 h, (s) 8 h and (t) 24 h. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Two-photon cellular nuclear imaging of B16-F10 cells using TAT-CDs (a), PI (b, e) and Trp/formic acid CDs (d). (c) Merged image of (a) and (b). (f) Merged image of (d) and (e). The excitation wavelength for CDs was 800 nm and 543 nm for PI. Scale bars = 20 μm.
Parameters of CDs obtained from seventeen kinds of amino acid and formic acid.

head.ce:title: Nuclear-targeted of TAT peptide-conjugated carbon dots for both one-and two-photon fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University, 467 Zhongshan Road, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Affiliated Hospital of Dalian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Dalian 116029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with both one- and two-photon fluorescence have attracted much attention in the field of bioimaging. In this study, we reported a strategy for the design of peptide-conjugated CDs for cellular nuclear-targeted imaging. The CDs were prepared from amino acid and formic acid via an one-step hydrothermal synthesis method, and then modified with trans-activator of transcription (TAT) peptide for both one- and two- photon nuclear-targeted fluorescence imaging. The CDs derived from tryptophan (Trp) and formic acid showed the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 58.4% with an average size about 1.7 nm in diameter. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the Trp/formic acid CDs were 360 and 442 nm, respectively. The Trp/formic acid CDs were successfully conjugated with TAT peptide through the reaction between the amino group of the peptide and carboxylic group of the CDs. Upon grafting with TAT peptide, the resulted TAT-Trp/formic acid CDs could be used as nuclear-targeted fluorescent probes for both one- and two-photon live cell fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular targeting fluorescence probes for cellular imaging have become invaluable tools in a variety of biological field because of their relatively high selectivity and easy maneuverability [1]. In recent years, two-photon fluorescent probes have received attraction in biological imaging because of their much deeper penetration, low photodamage and long observation time [2,3]. Considering the unique properties, fluorescent materials with two-photon fluorescent characteristics will undoubtedly open up a new way for the tracking of typical organelles in living cells [4]. For instance, in eukaryotic cells, the nuclear is the desirable targeting destination, which contains genetic components that determine the functions of the cells [5]. The variations of the nuclear organizational structure are closely related to disease phenotypes, and effective fluorescent probes of imaging cellular nuclear is crucial for disease diagnosis, nuclei tracing and disease treatment [6]. However, many key factors should be addressed in effective imaging of living cells, such as good organelle targeting capability, less phototoxicity and photobleaching effect of fluorescence probes [7,8]. Therefore, the development of nuclear-targeted fluorescent probes is expected to improve the efficiency of cell fluorescence imaging.

As a benign material, carbon is capable of forming various nanostructurtes, such as fullerene, carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds and carbon dots (CDs), due to its unique valency [9,10]. Among them, the CDs have been widely explored as fluoresce probes or markers for intracellular imaging due to their superior optical properties, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and unique photoluminescence [11,12]. The CDs have great potential for two-photon fluorescent imaging because of their upconversion photoluminescence properties, which have many hydrophilic surface functional groups and easily disperse in aqueous media without further surface modification compared with metal nanoparticles [13]. The abundant surface functional groups also make it possible for covalent attachment of the targeting moieties. The fluorescent CDs have many potential applications in bio-sensing, biomedicine, catalysis, and optoelectronic device in the literature [14,15]. However, the fluorescent CDs with two-photon fluorescent behavior for nuclear targeting are particularly challenging due to the distinct and specific structure of nucleus and selectivity of the targeting moieties [16,17]. Generally, specialized nuclear pore complexes are located in the nuclear membrane, which play an important role in controlling the transport of macromolecules from the cytoplasm to the nuclear. Interestingly, the trans-activator of transcription (TAT) peptide, containing 11-amino-acid within the HIV- 1 TAT protein, is able to act as nuclear localization signal domain and easily translocate both the plasma membrane and the nuclear membrane into the cellular nuclear [18]. It has been reported that the TAT peptide-mediated import process is not diﬀ ;usion-driven, and does not require ATP or cytosolic factors [19]. To increase the target specificity and improve cell permeation, TAT peptides have been successfully employed as targeting moieties onto the nanoparticle surface. For example, Pan et al. [5] proved that TAT peptide was an efficient molecule for transporting across the nuclear membrane via conjugating onto mesoporous silica nanoparticles. Roy et al. [20] have reported the encapsulation of CDs into TAT peptide-conjugated bubble nanoliposome carriers for multi-color cell-imaging and in vivo therapeutic delivery. Leung et al. [21] reported the dual function cationic nanodiamond delivery carriers with TAT peptide modification for efficient gene therapy. We have demonstrated the synthesis of N-doped CDs from bovine serum albumin and formic acid for live cell nuclear imaging [22]. However, the research of highly efficient nuclear-targeting fluorescent probes is still lacking, and the development of nanoprobes with highly fluorescent quantum yield for both one- and two-photon fluorescent nuclear-targeted imaging is highly desired for effective organelle tracking.

In this study, seventeen kinds of free amino acids were explored as raw materials for hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent CDs. The CDs prepared though the reaction of tryptophan (Trp) and formic acid with the highest fluorescent quantum yield were selected for preparation of TAT-conjugated fluorescent CDs (TAT-CDs) for both one- and two-photon nuclear-targeted fluorescence imaging. The microstructure and chemical composition of the fluorescent CDs were analyzed, and the TAT-CDs showed good capability for nuclear-targeting identification. The novelty of this study is that an innovative route was proposed to design and prepare the nuclear-targeted CD probe for both one- and two-photon fluorescence imaging. This work not only demonstrated a representative strategy for the design of TAT-conjugated fluorescent CDs for nuclear targeting, but also provided significant insights on the development of cellular targeted probes.

Seventeen kinds of free amino acids (alanine, arginine, asparagine, aspartic acid, cysteine, glutamic acid, glycine, histidine, isoleucine, leucine, lysine, phenylalanine, serine, threonine, tryptophan, tyrosine, valine) were purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co. LTD. (Beijing, China). TAT peptide (GRKKRRQRRRPQ) was purchased from Minghao Biotechnology Co. LTD. (Wuhan, China). Acetic acid (CH3COOH, >99.5%) was purchased from Guanghua Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Formic acid (HCOOH, >88%) was purchased from Tianhe Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Formaldehyde (CH2O, 37–40%) was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Sephadex gel G-25 was purchased from GE Healthcare (Fairfield, San Diego, USA). Benzotriazol-1-yl-oxytripyrrolidinophosphonium, hexafluorophosphate (PyBOP), 1-hydroxybenzotriazole hydrate (HOBt), quinine sulphate, fluorescein, N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), N, N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) and N,N-Diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals and solvents used in this study were of analytical grade.

In a typical synthesis procedure of CDs, freeamino acids (2 g) was dissolved in 20 mL water, followed by adding formic acid with molar ratio of formic acid and amino acid 1:1. The mixture was hydrothermally heated in a Teflon autoclave at 200 °C for 10 h. As for the preparation of CDs from Trp/formaldehyde or Trp/acetic acid, the formic acid was replaced by formaldehyde or acetic acid accordingly. The brown solution was cooled to room temperature naturally, then filtered by a 0.22-μm membrane to remove large or agglomerated particles. The solution was concentrated and then purified with a Sephadex G-25 gel chromatographic column. The fluorescent fractions were collected, lyophilized and stored in dark before use.

The CDs derived from Trp/formic acid (205.7 mg), PyBop (32.3 mg) and HOBt (7.2 mg) were mixed with 2 mL of DMF, and then the mixture was put into 100 mL sand core hopper. After shaking for 2 h, TAT peptide on beads with free amino groups on N-terminal (103 mg), 200 μL of DIPEA were added to the CDs DMF solution, then shaken for 48 h to form TAT-CDs conjugate. After shaking for 2 h, the resin beads were completely washed with DMF, methylene chloride, ether and water to remove the excess reactants. The conjugate of TAT-CDs was cleaved with 2 mL trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) solution (TFA 90%, thioanisole 5%, phenol 5% and water). The filtrate was isolated and added dropwise to 20 mL of ether. The white precipitate was obtained and washed for three times by ether to remove the impurities. To further purify the TAT-CDs, fluorescence components were collected by Sephadex G-25 gel chromatographic column, lyophilized and stored at −20 °C before use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were measured by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with electron energy of 200 kV. The fluorescence spectra were obtained by a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, MA, USA) with a xenon lamp as a light source. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a Frontier FTIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Norwalk, USA) with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were used to characterize the chemical composition using an Escalab 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation. The absorption spectra were obtained at room temperature by a Shimadzu UV2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was measured by a Horiba Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer with a 376 nm laser as the excitation source (Horiba Scientific, USA). Cell fluorescence imaging was performed using a laser confocal fluorescent microscope (Olympus microscope FV1000, Olympus, Japan) with a 40x objective lens in xy mode at 800 × 800 pixel resolution.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated by quinine sulfate (QY = 55%) as a reference. QY (Φ) was calculated according to the following equation:
Φ =
Φ '
×
A '
I '
×
A I
×
n 2
n
' 2
where Φ is the QY of the CDs, Φ’ refers to the QY of quinine sulfate. I and I’ are the integrated emission intensity of CDs and quinine sulfate. A and A’ is the optical density of CDs and quinine sulfate. n and n’ are the refraction index of the CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution with pH 2–11 was chosen as pH buffer solution for stability experiment. 200 μL CDs aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) was added to the BR buffer with different pH values (pH = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11) with a final volume of 2 mL. After sufficient mixing, the fluorescence intensity of the BR solution was recorded by a LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer using 360 nm wavelength as excitation. All the fluorescence intensity values were shown as a mean and standard error from triplicate measurements.

The CDs fluorescence intensity (1 mg mL−1) was recorded under the excitation wavelength 405 nm laser with FV1000 fluorescence microscope using fluorescein (1 μg mL−1) as a control. The fluorescence intensity values were measured for 300 s for comparison of the photostability change after normalization of corresponding initial value.

Mouse melanoma B16-F10 cancer cells were seeded in a micro cell culture dish special for a laser scanning confocal microscope at a density of 2 × 105 cells mL−1 in RPMI1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS, penicillin (100 units mL−1) and streptomycin (100 μg mL−1). After incubation in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h, the B16-F10 cells were treated with CDs, TAT-CDs or propidium iodide (PI) at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1, respectively, using untreated cells as blank control. PI staining was used for indicating of cell nuclear localization. After washing three times with phosphate buffer saline buffer to remove free CDs or TAT-CDs, cellular imaging was performed at 0, 2, 4, 8 and 24 h by an Olympus laser scanning confocal microscope. The excitation laser wavelength was 405, 488, and 543 nm, respectively, with corresponding emission wavelength in the ranges of 425–475, 500–530, and 560–660 nm. For two-photon fluorescent cellular imaging, an 800 nm laser (15% power) was used for excitation and the two-photon emitted light was collected between 380–560 nm and 575–630 nm, respectively.

Fluorescent CDs were obtained through a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using seventeen kinds of amino acids and formic acid as reaction precursors. The maximum excitation wavelengths of the obtained CDs with formic acid were in the range of 300–370 nm (Table 1 ). The maximum emission wavelength for the seventeen kinds of CDs are within the range of 440–472 nm under the excitation wavelength of 800 nm. As for the one-photoemission, the QY of CDs derived from amino acids with formic acid were from 5 to 58.4%, and the Trp/formic acid CDs showed the highest value of 58.4%. The excitation wavelength of Trp/formic acid CDs was 360 nm, which has the maximum one-photon emission at wavelength of 442 nm, and two-photon fluorescent at 450 nm. The CDs with high QY are very useful for the applications in biological imaging, drug and gene delivery [23]. Therefore, the tryptophan and formic acid could act as a good precursor for preparation of high QY fluorescent CDs.

To further explore whether formic acid is a suitable precursor for synthesis of high QY CDs, the formaldehyde or acetic acid was also used to react with Trp acid. Exhilaratingly, all three types of CDs exhibited upconversion fluorescence behaviour (Fig. 1 ). The maximum excitation wavelength of Trp/formic acid CDs, Trp/formaldehyde CDs and Trp/acetic acid CDs was 360 nm (Fig. 1a), 390 nm (Fig. 1c) and 370 nm (Fig. 1e), respectively, and the corresponding maximum two-photon fluorescent emission wavelength at 443, 449 and 446 nm. There is a shoulder in the spectra of TAT-CDs, as compared with that of CDs in Fig. 1a. It is because that the different surface states in TAT-CDs, leading to peak splits in a wide emission spectrum. All the CDs showed the maximum emission wavelengths of upconversion fluorescence at 450 nm. However, the QY of Trp/formaldehyde CDs and Trp/acetic acid were only 43.6% and 27.7%, which were much lower than that of the Trp/formic acid CDs of 58.4%. Therefore, the Trp/formic acid CDs were chosen for preparation of the TAT-CDs nano probes in the following experiments.

The XPS analysis can further characterize the properties of chemical bonds and functional groups on the surface of Trp/formic acid CDs. In a full scan XPS spectrum of Trp/formic acid CDs (Fig. 2 a), the signal peaks at 285, 532 and 399 eV are assigned to C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively, indicating that the surface of Trp/formic acid CDs is mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements [24]. In the high resolution C1s XPS spectra of Trp/formic acid CDs (inset of Fig. 2a), three peaks at 284.5, 286.8 and 288.05 eV indicate the presence of CC, CN and CO bonds, respectively. As an intrinsic characteristic of CDs, fluorescence lifetime shows the period of CDs remaining in its excited state prior to returning to ground state. The non-mono exponential decay of the one-photon fluorescence emission for Trp/formic acid CDs was shown in Fig. 2b. The average fluorescence lifetime is 14.23 ns for Trp/formic acid CDs as assessed by time-resolved photoluminescence measurements, indicating that the radiative recombination of the excitons results in fluorescence enhancement [25,26].

The stability of Trp/formic acid CDs at different pH value is of importance for the application in the biological imaging. The fluorescence intensity of the Trp/formic acid CDs showed a significantly gradient descent with the pH value increased from 2 to 11, but the emission wavelengths had no obvious shifting (Fig. 2c). This indicated that the radiative recombination of the energy-trapping sites on the Trp/formic acid CDs is environment-sensitive when pH changed from acidic to alkaline. It was possibly due to the preparation of Trp/formic acid CDs in an acidic environment during the deprotonation and deprotonation process, causing electrostatic doping/charging to the Trp/formic acid CDs and changing their Fermi level [27,28]. In addition, the fluorescence of Trp/formic acid CDs has no obvious reduction of intensity after being irritated with a 405 nm exciting light for 350 s (Fig. 2d), whereas that of fluorescein intensity decreased to 71% under the same condition. This indicated that the Trp/formic acid CDs solution showed excellent photostability compared with the conventional organic dyes of fluorescein. The good photostability of the Trp/formic acid CDs is a superiority to be used as fluorescent probes in more harsh conditions [29].

The nuclear localization signal can trigger the intra-nuclear transport of large heterogeneous nanoparticles [30]. Several nuclear targeting delivery strategies have been developed by modifying the surfaces of nanoparticles with different nuclear localization signal peptides, such as SV40T antigen, HIV-1 TAT peptide and adenoviral [6,31,32]. In these nuclear localization signal peptides, TAT peptide acted as an effective molecule to bind import receptors importin α and β (karyopherin) for translocating nanoparticles into cell nuclei and subsequently targeting the nuclear pore complexes of cell [5]. The surface of Trp/formic acid CDs were modified with TAT peptide, via the amino group of the peptide reacting with carboxylic group of the CDs [22], for one- and two-photon nuclear-targeting fluorescence imaging. After purifying through Sephadex G-25 gel column, the Trp/formic acid CDs and TAT-CDs morphology were characterized by TEM. The Trp/formic acid CDs are well dispersed and have an average size about 1.7 nm in diameter (Fig. 3 a). Corresponding particle size followed a normal distribution within the range from 1.0–2.6 nm (Fig. 3b). High-resolution TEM image further shows that the CDs are mostly non-crystal particles without obvious lattice fringes (inset of Fig. 3a), which is consistent with previous studies [20]. The TEM image of TAT-CDs shows that the TAT peptide conjugation resulted in the production of larger amorphous nanoparticles (Fig. 3c) with an average size of 6.5 nm (Fig. 3d). The enlarged nanoparticles strongly indicated the presence of the TAT peptide on the surface of the Trp/formic acid CDs. The dispersion of the TAT-CDs was not good as that of the Trp/formic acid CDs, which had aggregation visible to the naked eyes. This behavior was possible due to the like-charge repulsion between the Arg and Lys in the TAT peptide, indicating that after CD surface modification the TAT peptides were more likely to have a relatively uniform distribution on the surface of CD to avoid repulsive contacts [33].

Subsequently, the optical properties including fluorescence, UV–vis absorption and FT-IR spectrum of the TAT-CDs were examined to further characterize the obtained imaging agent. The fluorescence spectra of TAT-CDs in Fig. 4 a show an excitation-dependent feature with the emission maximum at 320 nm like most of previously reported CDs. The maximum excitation wavelength changed from 360 nm of Trp/formic acid CDs to 320 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was 428 nm with a blue shift of 15 nm (Fig. 1a). UV–vis absorption spectra of Trp/formic acid CDs and TAT-CDs (Fig. 4b) reveal that the CDs both exhibit two absorption peaks at 218 and 276 nm, which was ascribed to the π-π* (aromatic CC) and n-π* (CO, NO, CS, NN) transition [34]. After grafting TAT peptide, the 276 nm absorption peak disappeared, indicating successful conjugation of TAT peptides onto Trp/formic acid CDs. As shown in Fig. 4c, the FT-IR spectra reveal that Trp/formic acid CDs mainly contain amine (3422 cm−1), methylene (2964 and 2915 cm−1), amide carbonyl (1630 cm−1), CC (1485 cm−1), CN (1395 and 1340 cm−1), aromatic CO (1112 cm−1) functional groups [35]. The characteristic absorption peaks of TAT peptide were shown after the surface modification of Trp/formic acid CDs. Upon grafting TAT peptide, the characteristic peak of methylene at 2964 and 2915 cm-1 were disappeared and the absorption band at 1630 cm-1 shifted to 1656 cm-1. The FT-IR spectrum of TAT-CDs is much similar to that of TAT peptide, indicating that the Trp/formic acid CDs had been successfully conjugated with TAT peptide. The FT-IR spectra of Trp/formic acid CDs were consistent with the results of XPS analysis.

In vitro bioimaging of B16-F10 cells was carried out to check the capability of TAT-CDs for one- and two-photon live cell imaging using a confocal fluorescent microscope. Fig. 5 shows the bright-field and one-photon confocal fluorescent images of the B16-F10 cells incubated with 1 mg mL−1 of Trp/formic acid CDs, TAT-CDs and PI for 4 h. In the row of Fig. 5, the B16-F10 cells were only stained with PI to show the localization of cell nuclei. The bright-field image displays an organized concave-convex surface, indicating the morphology of the B16-F10 cells. The fluorescence of images showed that the B16-F10 cells emitted brilliant blue, green and red light under the excitation wavelength of 405, 488 and 543 nm, respectively. It is noteworthy that the fluorescent signal of Trp/formic acid CDs mainly distributed within the cell cytoplasm, without invading the nuclear (Fig. 5e.) similar with our previous results [36,37]. However, clear fluorescence signal was observed from nuclei in the B16-F10 cells upon incubation with 1 mg mL−1 of TAT-CDs for 4 h (Fig. 5g, 5 h and 5i), revealing that the TAT-CDs could enter into cellular and bind with nuclei. In comparison, the control cells stained with PI only showed fluorescence signal in cell nuclei (Fig. 5n and 5o) and no signals under the excitation of 405 and 488 nm. Therefore, it can be concluded that the prepared TAT-CDs have potential as fluorescent probes in nuclear targeting cellular imaging.

In consideration of the dynamic fluorescence change of the TAT-CDs in cell nuclear imaging, the accumulation of TAT-CDs in the B16-F10 cancer cells was investigated. The images of B16-F10 cancer cells treated with TAT-CDs/PI for different time periods were shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence signal from TAT-CDs in cellular nuclear was monitored for 0, 2, 4, 8, 24 h (Fig. 6a–e). The fluorescence signal from TAT-CDs can be observed from 2 to 24 h with its maximum intensity at 8 h. Similar phenomenon was also observed when the TAT-CDs were excited at 488 nm (Fig. 6f–j). PI, selectively bind to nucleic acids, can be clearly seen as the accumulation of red fluorescence excited at 535 nm [38] in the cell nuclear for 2, 4, 8, 24 h (Fig. 6k–o). The merged images (Fig. 6p-f) from the overlay of the three fluorescence channels certified that the TAT-CDs were located in same area of B16-F10 cancer cells nuclear as shown by PI. Meanwhile, the fluorescence signal of TAT-CDs continued for 24 h at cells nuclear. This may be due to that the TAT-CDs were able to escape from the lysosomes and subsequently accumulate in the nuclear [39]. Therefore, the TAT-CDs has great potential as nuclear-targeted fluorescent probes for live-cell fluorescence imaging.

The two-photon cellular nuclear-targeting imaging with the prepared TAT-CDs using non-targeted Trp/formic acid CDs as a control was shown in Fig. 7 . PI stained cells were included for better nuclear localization (Fig. 7b and e). The fluorescence of TAT-CDs was clearly shown in the cell nuclei (Fig. 7a) which was same as the PI signal displayed in (Fig. 7b). The overlay of the TAT-CDs and PI images (Fig. 7c) demonstrated that the TAT-CDs can stain the same position as shown by PI in the two-photon fluorescent fluorescent imaging. However, there was no signal of the cellular nuclei for the Trp/formic acid-CDs without TAT peptide modification (Fig. 7d) and only PI signal was found in the nuclei of overlay image (Fig. 7f). In addition, the two-photon fluorescent imaging of TAT-CDs was much clear than one-photon fluorescence imaging in recognizing nuclei details [40]. The results indicated that TAT-CDs have great potential to be use as the two-photon fluorescent nuclear-targeted probe in cellular imaging, and they can be further applied to high-contrast two-photon fluorescent cellular-specific bioimaging.

In summary, the CDs were facilely prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method using tryptophan and formic acid as precursors after screening from seventeen amino acids, and TAT-CDs were constructed for nuclear-targeting cellular imaging for the first time. The Trp/formic acid CDs showed strong one- and two-photon induced fluorescence, which can be used for both one- and two-photon fluorescence cellular imaging. The TAT-CDs showed good capability in nuclear-targeted imaging. We anticipate that the two-photon-induced fluorescent CDs modified with TAT peptide might have a promising application for nuclear-targeted fluorescence imaging.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915).
